name: Competitive Stone Skipping
description: Competitive stone skipping elevates the childhood pastime into a discipline governed by physics: the ideal stone weighs 3-8 ounces, is 1/4 inch thick, and must be launched at roughly 25 mph with a spin rate of about 14 revolutions per second at a 20-degree attack angle. Kurt Steiner holds the Guinness World Record at 88 skips, set in 2013 at Red Bridge, Pennsylvania, after collecting over 10,000 candidate stones sorted by type.

thumbnail.alt: Stone skipping across a calm river surface with multiple visible bounces

media.title: Why It's Almost Impossible to Skip a Stone 89 Times - WIRED

sources.title: Most skips of a skimming stone - Guinness World Records

time_to_competence.notes: Most people can learn to reliably skip a stone 5-10 times within a few hours of practice. The basic throwing motion is intuitive.

time_to_expert.notes: Steiner spent over a decade refining his technique from 40 skips (2002) to 88 (2013). Competitive-level consistency requires years of stone selection and biomechanical refinement.

feedback_delay.notes: You see and can count the result of every throw immediately. Each skip is individually visible and often audible.

failure_cost.notes: A bad throw simply sinks. No physical risk, no equipment damage. Stones are free. The only cost is repetitive strain from thousands of throws.

cognitive_demand.notes: Stone selection requires tactile assessment of weight, shape, and surface texture. Throw execution involves multiple simultaneous parameters: angle, speed, spin, and height.

physical_demand.notes: Requires a fast sidearm throw with precise wrist snap. Not exhausting but demands shoulder mobility, rotational power, and fine motor control.

prerequisite_knowledge.notes: Understanding hydrodynamics helps but is not required. Stone selection criteria can be learned empirically.

measurability.notes: Skips can be counted precisely. The metric is unambiguous: how many times did the stone touch the water?

perishability.notes: The throwing motion is relatively simple and retained well. Stone selection intuition may fade but recovers quickly with practice.

precision_required.notes: At world-record level, launch angle must be within a few degrees, spin rate within narrow tolerances, and the stone's orientation perfectly controlled.

ambiguity.notes: Completely objective. The stone either skipped or it did not. Slow-motion video resolves any counting disputes.

instruction_dependency.notes: The basic technique is self-teachable through trial and error. Advanced optimization benefits from physics knowledge but few formal coaches exist.

environmental_variability.notes: Wind, water current, surface chop, and available stone quality vary dramatically by location. Steiner specifically chose Red Bridge for its calm water.

concurrent_skills.notes: Stone assessment, body mechanics, and environmental reading happen simultaneously but the actual throw is a single coordinated action.

plateau_density.notes: Progress from 20 to 40 skips is much harder than 5 to 20. Beyond 50 skips, each additional skip requires exponentially better technique and stone selection.

domain_evolution.notes: The physics has not changed since Spallanzani's 18th-century explanation. NASSA was founded in 1989 but the competitive scene remains tiny.

observability.notes: A 5-skip throw versus an 88-skip throw is visually stunning. The latter produces a long, audible ripple trail that is immediately impressive.

luck_variance.notes: Stone quality varies even within a curated collection. Micro-currents and unseen debris can terminate a perfect throw. Weather is a factor.

social_coordination.notes: Purely individual. Competitions are solo performance. No partner or team component.

methodology_notes: World record data from Guinness World Records. Physics from Lyderic Bocquet's research on stone skipping hydrodynamics. Competition history from NASSA and stoneskipping.com.
